content:
# Numbers Chapter 20 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 172–173; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 127–128 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_020_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_020_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — DEATH, WATER, AND DEATH: THE CHIASTIC TRAGEDY OF NUMBERS 20:** Numbers 20 is framed by two deaths — Miriam at its opening (v. 1) and Aaron at its close (vv. 22–29) — with the crisis at the waters of Meribah at its center (vv. 2–13). This chapter marks a turning point in the wilderness narrative: the generation of the exodus is passing away, and even Moses and Aaron are told they will not enter the promised land. The chapter also contains the diplomatic exchange with Edom (vv. 14–21), in which Israel's "brother" refuses passage through his territory. The Leningrad Codex presents Numbers 20 across pages 172–173, beginning in the left column of page 172 (following the conclusion of chapter 19) and continuing onto page 173. The Vaticanus LXX preserves the chapter across pages 127–128 (pa_0127 to pa_0128), with the chapter division marked as ΚΑ (21 in the LXX numbering scheme). A parashah marker ס (closed section) appears before v. 1 in the Leningrad Codex, while a פ (open section) marker appears before v. 14, separating the Meribah narrative from the Edom embassy.

---

## The Death of Miriam and the Waters of Meribah (vv. 1–13)

**1** And-came [va-yavo'u] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah], the-wilderness of [midbar] Zin [Tsin] in-the-month [ba-khodesh] the-first [ha-ri'shon], and-dwelt [va-yeshev] the-people [ha-am] in-Kadesh [be-Qadesh]; and-died [va-tamot] Miriam [Miryam] there [sham], and-she-was-buried [va-tiqqaver] there [sham].

> **Key terms:** מִדְבַּר־צִן (midbar-Tsin) = "wilderness of Zin" — the southern boundary region of Canaan (Num 13:21; 34:3–4), distinct from the wilderness of Sin (סִין, Sin) near Sinai (Exod 16:1). בַּחֹדֶשׁ הָרִאשׁוֹן (ba-khodesh ha-ri'shon) = "in the first month" — the year is not specified. Rabbinic tradition (Seder Olam Rabbah 9) places this in the fortieth year of wandering, meaning nearly thirty-eight years have elapsed since the spy narrative of chapters 13–14. וַתָּמָת שָׁם מִרְיָם (va-tamot sham Miryam) = "and Miriam died there" — reported with stark brevity. No mourning period is recorded (contrast Aaron in v. 29 and Moses in Deut 34:8). The juxtaposition of Miriam's death with the subsequent water crisis led the rabbis to connect Miriam with the miraculous well that sustained Israel (b. Ta'anit 9a: "The well was given on account of Miriam"). **LXX:** καὶ ἦλθον οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ, πᾶσα ἡ συναγωγή, εἰς τὴν ἔρημον Σιν ἐν τῷ μηνὶ τῷ πρώτῳ, καὶ κατέμεινεν ὁ λαὸς ἐν Καδης· καὶ ἐτελεύτησεν ἐκεῖ Μαριαμ καὶ ἐτάφη ἐκεῖ ("and the sons of Israel came, all the congregation, into the wilderness of Sin in the first month, and the people remained in Kadesh; and Miriam died there and was buried there").

**2** And-there-was-no [ve-lo-hayah] water [mayim] for-the-congregation [la-edah]; and-they-assembled [va-yiqqahalu] against [al] Moses [Mosheh] and-against [ve-al] Aaron [Aharon].

> **Key terms:** וְלֹא־הָיָה מַיִם לָעֵדָה (ve-lo-hayah mayim la-edah) = "and there was no water for the congregation" — the immediate sequel to Miriam's death reinforces the rabbinic association of Miriam with the well. The narrative structure implies causation: she dies, the water ceases. וַיִּקָּהֲלוּ (va-yiqqahalu) = "and they assembled" — the niphal of קהל (qahal), suggesting a hostile gathering. The same verb describes the rebellion of Korah (Num 16:3, 19; 17:7). **LXX:** καὶ οὐκ ἦν ὕδωρ τῇ συναγωγῇ· καὶ ἐπισυνέστησαν ἐπὶ Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων ("and there was no water for the congregation; and they gathered together against Moses and Aaron").

**3** And-contended [va-yarev] the-people [ha-am] with [im] Moses [Mosheh], and-they-spoke [va-yomeru] saying [le'mor]: "And-if-only [ve-lu] we-had-expired [gava'nu] when-expired [bi-geva] our-brothers [akheinu] before [lifnei] YHWH!

> **Key terms:** וַיָּרֶב הָעָם (va-yarev ha-am) = "and the people contended" — from the root ריב (riv, "to contend/strive/dispute"), the same root that gives the location its name מְרִיבָה (Merivah, "contention") in v. 13. וְלוּ גָוַעְנוּ (ve-lu gava'nu) = "and if only we had expired" — the particle לוּ (lu) introduces a counterfactual wish; גָּוַע (gava) means "to expire/breathe one's last" (Gen 6:17; 7:21; 25:8). בִּגְוַע אַחֵינוּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (bi-geva akheinu lifnei YHWH) = "when our brothers expired before YHWH" — referring to those who died in the plague after Korah's rebellion (Num 17:14 [ET 16:49]), where fourteen thousand seven hundred perished. **LXX:** καὶ ἐλοιδόρησεν ὁ λαὸς πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγοντες· ὄφελον ἀπεθάνομεν ἐν τῇ ἀπωλείᾳ τῶν ἀδελφῶν ἡμῶν ἔναντι κυρίου ("and the people reviled Moses, saying: would that we had died in the destruction of our brothers before [the] Lord").

**4** And-why [ve-lammah] have-you-brought [heve'tem] [et] the-assembly of [qehal] YHWH unto [el] the-wilderness [ha-midbar] the-this [ha-zeh] to-die [lamut] there [sham], we [anakhnu] and-our-livestock [u-ve'irenu]?

> **Key terms:** קְהַל יְהוָה (qehal YHWH) = "the assembly of YHWH" — despite their rebellion, the people identify themselves with the covenantal title, the assembled people of YHWH. הֲבֵאתֶם (heve'tem) = "have you brought" — the hiphil of בוא (bo'), addressing both Moses and Aaron as jointly responsible for leading them into the wilderness. The complaint echoes earlier murmurings (Exod 16:3; 17:3; Num 11:4–6; 14:2–3). אֲנַחְנוּ וּבְעִירֵנוּ (anakhnu u-ve'irenu) = "we and our livestock" — the addition of livestock intensifies the complaint; even the animals suffer. **LXX:** καὶ ἵνα τί ἀνηγάγετε τὴν συναγωγὴν κυρίου εἰς τὴν ἔρημον ταύτην ἀποκτεῖναι ἡμᾶς καὶ τὰ κτήνη ἡμῶν; ("and why have you brought the assembly of [the] Lord into this wilderness to kill us and our cattle?").

**5** And-why [ve-lammah] have-you-brought-us-up [he'elitunu] from-Egypt [mi-Mitsrayim] to-bring [le-havi] [et]-us [otanu] unto [el] the-place [ha-maqom] the-evil [ha-ra] the-this [ha-zeh]? Not [lo] a-place of [meqom] seed [zera] and-fig [u-te'enah] and-vine [va-gefen] and-pomegranate [ve-rimmon]; and-water [u-mayim] there-is-none [ayin] to-drink [lishtot]."

> **Key terms:** הֶעֱלִיתֻנוּ (he'elitunu) = "have you brought us up" — the hiphil of עלה (alah, "to go up"), with the first-person plural suffix. The verb implies ascent from Egypt, but the complaint reframes it as a cruel extraction. הַמָּקוֹם הָרָע הַזֶּה (ha-maqom ha-ra ha-zeh) = "this evil place" — the wilderness is deemed "evil" not morally but in the sense of "bad, worthless, unfit." לֹא מְקוֹם זֶרַע וּתְאֵנָה וְגֶפֶן וְרִמּוֹן (lo meqom zera u-te'enah va-gefen ve-rimmon) = "not a place of seed and fig and vine and pomegranate" — a fourfold enumeration of agricultural abundance, contrasting the wilderness with the promised land's fertility (cf. Deut 8:8). וּמַיִם אַיִן לִשְׁתּוֹת (u-mayim ayin lishtot) = "and water there is none to drink" — the climactic complaint returns to the triggering crisis. **LXX:** καὶ ἵνα τί τοῦτο; ἀνηγάγετε ἡμᾶς ἐξ Αἰγύπτου παραγενέσθαι εἰς τὸν τόπον τὸν πονηρὸν τοῦτον· τόπος οὗ οὐ σπείρεται οὐδὲ συκαῖ οὐδὲ ἄμπελοι οὐδὲ ῥοαί, οὐδὲ ὕδωρ ἐστὶν πιεῖν ("and why is this? you brought us up from Egypt to come to this evil place — a place where there is no sowing, nor figs, nor vines, nor pomegranates, nor is there water to drink").

**6** And-came [va-yavo] Moses [Mosheh] and-Aaron [ve-Aharon] from-before [mi-penei] the-assembly [ha-qahal] unto [el] the-entrance of [petakh] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed], and-they-fell [va-yippelu] upon [al] their-faces [peneihem]; and-appeared [va-yera] the-glory of [kevod] YHWH unto-them [aleihem].

> **Key terms:** מִפְּנֵי הַקָּהָל (mi-penei ha-qahal) = "from before the assembly" — Moses and Aaron withdraw from the hostile congregation and approach the sacred space. אֶל־פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (el-petakh Ohel Mo'ed) = "to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting" — the threshold between the profane camp and the divine presence. וַיִּפְּלוּ עַל־פְּנֵיהֶם (va-yippelu al-peneihem) = "and they fell upon their faces" — a posture of prostration, intercession, and desperation (cf. Num 14:5; 16:4, 22; 17:10). וַיֵּרָא כְבוֹד־יְהוָה (va-yera kevod-YHWH) = "and the glory of YHWH appeared" — the divine response to their prostration, paralleling Num 14:10; 16:19; 17:7. **LXX:** καὶ ἦλθεν Μωυσῆς καὶ Ααρων ἀπὸ προσώπου τῆς συναγωγῆς ἐπὶ τὴν θύραν τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου καὶ ἔπεσαν ἐπὶ πρόσωπον· καὶ ὤφθη ἡ δόξα κυρίου πρὸς αὐτούς ("and Moses and Aaron came from before the congregation to the door of the tent of testimony and fell on [their] face; and the glory of [the] Lord appeared to them").

**7** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**8** "Take [qakh] [et] the-staff [ha-matteh], and-assemble [ve-haqhel] [et] the-congregation [ha-edah], you [attah] and-Aaron [ve-Aharon] your-brother [akhikha], and-speak [ve-dibbartem] unto [el] the-rock [ha-sela] before-their-eyes [le-eineihem], and-it-shall-give [ve-natan] its-waters [meimav]; and-you-shall-bring-out [ve-hotse'ta] for-them [lahem] water [mayim] from [min] the-rock [ha-sela], and-you-shall-give-drink [ve-hishqita] [et] the-congregation [ha-edah] and-[et] their-livestock [be'iram]."

> **Key terms:** קַח אֶת־הַמַּטֶּה (qakh et-ha-matteh) = "take the staff" — the divine command specifies taking the staff but speaking to the rock, not striking it. This distinction becomes critical for the transgression in v. 11. הַסֶּלַע (ha-sela) = "the rock" — the word סֶלַע (sela) denotes a large cliff-face or crag, distinct from צוּר (tsur, "rock/boulder") used in the parallel Exodus narrative (Exod 17:6). וְדִבַּרְתֶּם אֶל־הַסֶּלַע (ve-dibbartem el-ha-sela) = "and you shall speak to the rock" — the plural verb addresses both Moses and Aaron. The command is to speak, not to strike. וְנָתַן מֵימָיו (ve-natan meimav) = "and it shall give its waters" — the rock is personified as an agent that yields water in response to speech. **LXX:** λαβὲ τὴν ῥάβδον ... καὶ λαλήσατε πρὸς τὴν πέτραν ἔναντι αὐτῶν, καὶ δώσει τὰ ὕδατα αὐτῆς ("take the rod ... and speak to the rock before them, and it shall give its waters").

**9** And-took [va-yiqqakh] Moses [Mosheh] [et] the-staff [ha-matteh] from-before [mi-lifnei] YHWH, as-which [ka'asher] he-commanded-him [tsivvahu].

> **Key terms:** מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה (mi-lifnei YHWH) = "from before YHWH" — the staff was kept in the sanctuary, before the divine presence. This is likely "the staff of Aaron" that budded (Num 17:25 [ET 17:10]), placed before the Testimony as a sign. כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּהוּ (ka'asher tsivvahu) = "as he commanded him" — Moses obeys the first part of the command (taking the staff) faithfully. **LXX:** καὶ ἔλαβεν Μωυσῆς τὴν ῥάβδον τὴν ἀπέναντι κυρίου, καθὰ συνέταξεν κύριος ("and Moses took the rod from before [the] Lord, as [the] Lord commanded").

**10** And-assembled [va-yaqhilu] Moses [Mosheh] and-Aaron [ve-Aharon] [et] the-assembly [ha-qahal] unto [el] the-face of [penei] the-rock [ha-sela], and-he-said [va-yomer] to-them [lahem]: "Hear [shim'u] now [na], the-rebels [ha-morim]! From [ha-min] the-rock [ha-sela] the-this [ha-zeh] shall-we-bring-out [notsi] for-you [lakhem] water [mayim]?"

> **Key terms:** הַמֹּרִים (ha-morim) = "the rebels" — from the root מרה (marah, "to rebel/be contentious"). Moses addresses the congregation with a sharp rebuke. The harshness of this address may be part of his transgression. הֲמִן־הַסֶּלַע הַזֶּה נוֹצִיא לָכֶם מָיִם (ha-min-ha-sela ha-zeh notsi lakhem mayim) = "from this rock shall we bring out for you water?" — the rhetorical question is ambiguous: does "we" refer to Moses and Aaron (implying they, not God, produce the miracle), or is it an expression of exasperation? The first-person plural נוֹצִיא (notsi, "shall we bring out") may be the crux of the transgression — attributing the miracle to human agency rather than divine power. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξεκκλησίασεν Μωυσῆς καὶ Ααρων τὴν συναγωγὴν ἀπέναντι τῆς πέτρας, καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς· ἀκούσατέ μου, οἱ ἀπειθεῖς· μὴ ἐκ τῆς πέτρας ταύτης ἐξάξομεν ὑμῖν ὕδωρ; ("and Moses and Aaron assembled the congregation before the rock, and he said to them: hear me, you disobedient ones! Shall we bring out for you water from this rock?").

**11** And-raised [va-yarem] Moses [Mosheh] [et] his-hand [yado], and-he-struck [va-yakh] [et] the-rock [ha-sela] with-his-staff [be-mattehu] twice [pa'amayim]; and-came-out [va-yetse'u] water [mayim] abundant [rabbim], and-drank [va-tesht] the-congregation [ha-edah] and-their-livestock [u-ve'iram].

> **Key terms:** וַיָּרֶם מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יָדוֹ (va-yarem Mosheh et-yado) = "and Moses raised his hand" — the singular יָדוֹ (yado, "his hand") is noteworthy; some versions read the dual יָדָיו (yadav, "his hands"). The Leningrad Codex reads the singular. וַיַּךְ אֶת־הַסֶּלַע בְּמַטֵּהוּ פַּעֲמָיִם (va-yakh et-ha-sela be-mattehu pa'amayim) = "and he struck the rock with his staff twice" — the command was to speak to the rock (v. 8), but Moses struck it — and struck it twice, suggesting either anger or doubt that the first blow would suffice. מַיִם רַבִּים (mayim rabbim) = "abundant water" — despite the transgression, the miracle occurs. God's faithfulness to the people is not contingent on Moses' obedience. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπάρας Μωυσῆς τὴν χεῖρα αὐτοῦ ἐπάταξεν τὴν πέτραν τῇ ῥάβδῳ δίς· καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ὕδωρ πολύ, καὶ ἔπιεν ἡ συναγωγὴ καὶ τὰ κτήνη αὐτῶν ("and Moses, raising his hand, struck the rock with the rod twice; and much water came out, and the congregation and their cattle drank").

**12** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh] and-unto [ve-el] Aaron [Aharon]: "Because [ya'an] not [lo] you-believed [he'emantem] in-me [bi] to-sanctify-me [le-haqdisheni] before-the-eyes of [le-einei] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], therefore [lakhen] not [lo] you-shall-bring [tavi'u] [et] the-assembly [ha-qahal] the-this [ha-zeh] unto [el] the-land [ha-arets] which [asher] I-have-given [natatti] to-them [lahem]."

> **Key terms:** יַעַן לֹא־הֶאֱמַנְתֶּם בִּי (ya'an lo-he'emantem bi) = "because you did not believe in me" — the hiphil of אמן (aman, "to be firm/faithful"), meaning "to trust, to put faith in." The charge is a failure of faith, not merely a procedural violation. לְהַקְדִּישֵׁנִי (le-haqdisheni) = "to sanctify me" — the hiphil of קדשׁ (q-d-sh) with first-person suffix: "to make me holy" in the sight of the people. By striking rather than speaking, Moses failed to display God's holiness — the power of the divine word alone. לָכֵן לֹא תָבִיאוּ (lakhen lo tavi'u) = "therefore you shall not bring" — the sentence falls on both Moses and Aaron: exclusion from the promised land. This is the definitive verdict on the leaders of the exodus generation. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων· ὅτι οὐκ ἐπιστεύσατέ μοι ἁγιάσαι με ἐναντίον τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ, διὰ τοῦτο οὐκ εἰσάξετε τὴν συναγωγὴν ταύτην εἰς τὴν γῆν ἣν δέδωκα αὐτοῖς ("and [the] Lord said to Moses and Aaron: because you did not believe me to sanctify me before the sons of Israel, therefore you shall not bring this congregation into the land which I have given them").

**13** These [hemah] [are] the-waters of [mei] Meribah [Merivah], where [asher] contended [ravu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] with [et] YHWH, and-he-was-sanctified [va-yiqqadesh] among-them [bam].

> **Key terms:** מֵי מְרִיבָה (mei Merivah) = "the waters of Meribah/Contention" — from the root ריב (riv, "to contend"), the same root as the people's quarrel in v. 3. The place is named for the strife. אֲשֶׁר־רָבוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־יְהוָה (asher-ravu benei-Yisra'el et-YHWH) = "where the Sons of Israel contended with YHWH" — their quarrel with Moses is reframed as a quarrel with God. וַיִּקָּדֵשׁ בָּם (va-yiqqadesh bam) = "and he was sanctified among them" — the niphal of קדשׁ (q-d-sh). Despite the human failure, YHWH's holiness was manifested — through the miracle of water, through the judgment upon Moses and Aaron, or both. The paradox: the leaders failed to sanctify God (v. 12), yet God was sanctified nonetheless (v. 13). **LXX:** τοῦτο ὕδωρ ἀντιλογίας, ὅτι ἐλοιδορήθησαν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἔναντι κυρίου, καὶ ἡγιάσθη ἐν αὐτοῖς ("this is the water of contradiction, because the sons of Israel reviled before [the] Lord, and he was sanctified among them").

---

## The Embassy to Edom (vv. 14–21)

**14** And-sent [va-yishlakh] Moses [Mosheh] messengers [mal'akhim] from-Kadesh [mi-Qadesh] unto [el] the-king of [melekh] Edom [Edom]: "Thus [koh] says [amar] your-brother [akhikha] Israel [Yisra'el]: You [attah] know [yadata] [et] all [kol] the-hardship [ha-tela'ah] which [asher] has-found-us [metsa'atnu].

> **Key terms:** מַלְאָכִים (mal'akhim) = "messengers" — the same word used for angels; here it denotes human envoys. אָחִיךָ יִשְׂרָאֵל (akhikha Yisra'el) = "your brother Israel" — the kinship claim is foundational: Israel (Jacob) and Edom (Esau) are twin brothers (Gen 25:24–26). The diplomatic address invokes the fraternal bond to appeal for safe passage. הַתְּלָאָה (ha-tela'ah) = "the hardship" — a rare noun (only here, Exod 18:8, Lam 3:5, Neh 9:32), denoting exhaustion, weariness, and affliction. **LXX:** καὶ ἀπέστειλεν Μωυσῆς ἀγγέλους ἐκ Καδης πρὸς βασιλέα Εδωμ λέγων· τάδε λέγει ὁ ἀδελφός σου Ισραηλ· σὺ ἐπίστῃ πάντα τὸν μόχθον τὸν εὑρόντα ἡμᾶς ("and Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to the king of Edom, saying: thus says your brother Israel: you know all the toil that has found us").

**15** And-went-down [va-yeredu] our-fathers [avoteinu] to-Egypt [Mitsraymah], and-we-dwelt [va-neshev] in-Egypt [be-Mitsrayim] days [yamim] many [rabbim]; and-dealt-evil [va-yare'u] to-us [lanu] Egypt [Mitsrayim] and-to-our-fathers [ve-la-avoteinu].

> **Key terms:** וַיֵּרְדוּ אֲבֹתֵינוּ מִצְרַיְמָה (va-yeredu avoteinu Mitsraymah) = "and our fathers went down to Egypt" — the locative ה- (-ah) on מִצְרַיִם indicates direction of movement. The historical recital begins with the patriarchal descent into Egypt. יָמִים רַבִּים (yamim rabbim) = "many days" — a compressed reference to the centuries of sojourn. וַיָּרֵעוּ לָנוּ מִצְרַיִם (va-yare'u lanu Mitsrayim) = "and Egypt dealt evil to us" — the hiphil of רעע (ra'a, "to be evil/do evil"). Egypt is personified as the agent of oppression. **LXX:** καὶ κατέβησαν οἱ πατέρες ἡμῶν εἰς Αἴγυπτον, καὶ παρῳκήσαμεν ἐν Αἰγύπτῳ ἡμέρας πλείους· καὶ ἐκάκωσαν ἡμᾶς οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι ("and our fathers went down to Egypt, and we sojourned in Egypt many days; and the Egyptians afflicted us").

**16** And-we-cried-out [va-nits'aq] unto [el] YHWH, and-he-heard [va-yishma] our-voice [qolenu], and-he-sent [va-yishlakh] a-messenger [mal'akh], and-he-brought-us-out [va-yotsi'enu] from-Egypt [mi-Mitsrayim]; and-behold [ve-hinneh] we [anakhnu] in-Kadesh [be-Qadesh], a-city [ir] the-edge of [qetseh] your-border [gevulekha].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּשְׁלַח מַלְאָךְ (va-yishlakh mal'akh) = "and he sent a messenger/angel" — the same word מַלְאָךְ (mal'akh) used for the human messengers in v. 14 now refers to a divine agent. Whether this is the angel of YHWH (cf. Exod 14:19; 23:20; 33:2) or a general reference to divine intervention is debated. עִיר קְצֵה גְבוּלֶךָ (ir qetseh gevulekha) = "a city at the edge of your border" — Kadesh is identified as situated at the boundary of Edomite territory, establishing the geographical basis for the request. **LXX:** καὶ ἀνεβοήσαμεν πρὸς κύριον, καὶ εἰσήκουσεν κύριος τῆς φωνῆς ἡμῶν, καὶ ἀποστείλας ἄγγελον ἐξήγαγεν ἡμᾶς ἐξ Αἰγύπτου· καὶ νῦν ἐσμεν ἐν Καδης, πόλει ἐκ μέρους τῶν ὁρίων σου ("and we cried out to [the] Lord, and [the] Lord heard our voice, and having sent an angel he brought us out of Egypt; and now we are in Kadesh, a city on the edge of your borders").

**17** Let-us-pass [na'averah] now [na] through-your-land [be-artsekha]; not [lo] we-shall-pass [na'avor] through-a-field [be-sadeh] and-through-a-vineyard [u-ve-kerem], and-not [ve-lo] we-shall-drink [nishteh] water of [mei] a-well [ve'er]; by-the-road [derekh] the-king [ha-melekh] we-shall-go [nelekh]; not [lo] we-shall-turn [nitteh] right [yamin] or-left [u-semo'l] until [ad] that [asher] we-pass-through [na'avor] your-border [gevulekha]."

> **Key terms:** נַעְבְּרָה־נָּא בְאַרְצֶךָ (na'averah-na ve-artsekha) = "let us pass, please, through your land" — the cohortative with נָא (na, "please/now") makes this a respectful request, not a demand. דֶּרֶךְ הַמֶּלֶךְ (derekh ha-melekh) = "the King's Highway" — a major international trade route running north-south through Transjordan (cf. Num 21:22). The promise to stay on this road and not deviate into fields or vineyards, or even drink from local wells, demonstrates the intent of peaceful transit. לֹא נִטֶּה יָמִין וּשְׂמֹאול (lo nitteh yamin u-semo'l) = "we shall not turn right or left" — a formula of strict adherence to a path (cf. Deut 2:27; 5:32; 17:11, 20; 28:14). **LXX:** παρελευσόμεθα διὰ τῆς γῆς σου· οὐ διελευσόμεθα δι᾽ ἀγρῶν οὐδὲ δι᾽ ἀμπελώνων, οὐδὲ πιόμεθα ὕδωρ ἐκ λάκκου σου· ὁδῷ βασιλικῇ πορευσόμεθα, οὐκ ἐκκλινοῦμεν δεξιὰ οὐδὲ εὐώνυμα, ἕως ἂν παρέλθωμεν τὰ ὅριά σου ("we shall pass through your land; we shall not go through fields or vineyards, nor drink water from your cistern; we shall go by the royal road, we shall not turn right or left, until we pass through your borders").

**18** And-said [va-yomer] unto-him [elav] Edom [Edom]: "Not [lo] you-shall-pass [ta'avor] through-me [bi], lest [pen] with-the-sword [ba-kherev] I-go-out [etse] to-meet-you [liqratekha]."

> **Key terms:** לֹא תַעֲבֹר בִּי (lo ta'avor bi) = "you shall not pass through me" — a flat refusal. The preposition בִּי (bi, "through me") personifies the nation as an individual, consistent with the identification of Edom with Esau. פֶּן־בַּחֶרֶב אֵצֵא לִקְרָאתֶךָ (pen-ba-kherev etse liqratekha) = "lest with the sword I go out to meet you" — a military threat. The brother threatens the brother with violence. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτὸν Εδωμ· οὐ διελεύσῃ δι᾽ ἐμοῦ· εἰ δὲ μή, ἐν πολέμῳ ἐξελεύσομαι εἰς συνάντησίν σοι ("and Edom said to him: you shall not pass through me; and if [you do], in war I shall come out to meet you").

**19** And-said [va-yomeru] unto-him [elav] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]: "By-the-highway [ba-mesillah] we-shall-go-up [na'aleh], and-if [ve-im] your-water [meimekha] we-drink [nishteh], I [ani] and-my-livestock [u-miqnai], then-I-shall-give [ve-natatti] their-price [mikhram]; only [raq] there-is-no [ein] thing [davar] — on-my-feet [be-raglai] let-me-pass-through [e'eberah]."

> **Key terms:** בַּמְּסִלָּה נַעֲלֶה (ba-mesillah na'aleh) = "by the highway we shall go up" — מְסִלָּה (mesillah) is a built-up, graded road (from סלל, salal, "to heap up/build up"), synonymous with the King's Highway. וְנָתַתִּי מִכְרָם (ve-natatti mikhram) = "and I shall give their price" — Israel offers to pay for any water consumed, removing even the pretense of economic burden. The shift from first-person plural to singular (אֲנִי, ani, "I") may reflect Moses speaking on behalf of the nation, or a diplomatic convention of national personification. רַק אֵין־דָּבָר בְּרַגְלַי אֶעֱבֹרָה (raq ein-davar be-raglai e'eberah) = "only — it is no thing — on my feet let me pass through" — a minimizing plea: "this is nothing; just let me walk through on foot." **LXX:** καὶ λέγουσιν αὐτῷ οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ· παρὰ τὸ ὄρος παρελευσόμεθα· ἐὰν δὲ τοῦ ὕδατός σου πίωμεν ἐγὼ καὶ τὰ κτήνη μου, δώσω τιμήν σοι· ἀλλὰ τὸ πρᾶγμα οὐδέν ἐστιν, παρὰ τὸ ὄρος παρελευσόμεθα ("and the sons of Israel say to him: by the mountain we shall pass; and if we drink your water, I and my cattle, I shall give you the price; but the matter is nothing, by the mountain we shall pass").

**20** And-he-said [va-yomer]: "Not [lo] you-shall-pass-through [ta'avor]!" And-came-out [va-yetse] Edom [Edom] to-meet-him [liqrato] with-a-people [be-am] heavy [kaved] and-with-a-hand [u-ve-yad] strong [khazaqah].

> **Key terms:** בְּעַם כָּבֵד וּבְיָד חֲזָקָה (be-am kaved u-ve-yad khazaqah) = "with a heavy people and with a strong hand" — a massive military force. The phrase יָד חֲזָקָה (yad khazaqah, "strong hand") is the same expression used for YHWH's deliverance of Israel from Egypt (Exod 3:19; 6:1; 13:9; 32:11; Deut 4:34; 5:15; 6:21; 7:8; 9:26; 26:8). The irony is bitter: Edom wields against his brother the same language of overwhelming force that YHWH used against Pharaoh. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν· οὐ διελεύσῃ δι᾽ ἐμοῦ. καὶ ἐξῆλθεν Εδωμ εἰς συνάντησιν αὐτῷ ἐν ὄχλῳ βαρεῖ καὶ ἐν χειρὶ ἰσχυρᾷ ("and he said: you shall not pass through me. And Edom came out to meet him with a heavy crowd and with a strong hand").

**21** And-refused [va-yema'en] Edom [Edom] to-give [ttet] [et] Israel [Yisra'el] to-pass [avor] through-his-border [bi-gevulo]; and-turned-away [va-yet] Israel [Yisra'el] from-upon-him [me-alav].

> **Key terms:** וַיְמָאֵן אֱדוֹם (va-yema'en Edom) = "and Edom refused" — the piel of מאן (ma'en, "to refuse"), expressing deliberate, emphatic refusal (cf. Gen 37:35; 39:8; Exod 7:14). תֵּת אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל עֲבֹר בִּגְבֻלוֹ (ttet et-Yisra'el avor bi-gevulo) = "to give Israel to pass through his border" — the infinitive construct תֵּת (ttet, "to give") governs the infinitive עֲבֹר (avor, "to pass"), creating the idiom "to allow passage." וַיֵּט יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵעָלָיו (va-yet Yisra'el me-alav) = "and Israel turned away from upon him" — Israel does not fight but detours. The restraint is notable: God does not command Israel to attack Edom (cf. Deut 2:4–6, where YHWH explicitly forbids aggression against Esau). **LXX:** καὶ οὐκ ἠθέλησεν Εδωμ δοῦναι τῷ Ισραηλ παρελθεῖν διὰ τῶν ὁρίων αὐτοῦ· καὶ ἐξέκλινεν Ισραηλ ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ("and Edom did not wish to allow Israel to pass through his borders; and Israel turned aside from him").

---

## The Death of Aaron on Mount Hor (vv. 22–29)

**22** And-journeyed [va-yis'u] from-Kadesh [mi-Qadesh]; and-came [va-yavo'u] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah], Mount [Hor] Hor [ha-Hor].

> **Key terms:** הֹר הָהָר (Hor ha-Har) = "Mount Hor" — literally "Hor the mountain." The double form (noun + definite noun) is unusual and likely preserves an archaic toponym. The location is traditionally identified near Petra in modern Jordan, though the precise site is debated. The Leningrad Codex clearly reads הֹר הָהָר with the definite article on הָהָר. **LXX:** καὶ ἀπῆραν ἐκ Καδης· καὶ παρεγένοντο οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ, πᾶσα ἡ συναγωγή, εἰς Ωρ τὸ ὄρος ("and they departed from Kadesh; and the sons of Israel came, all the congregation, to Hor the mountain").

**23** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh] and-unto [ve-el] Aaron [Aharon] at-Mount [be-Hor] Hor [ha-Hor], upon [al] the-border of [gevul] the-land of [erets] Edom [Edom], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** עַל־גְּבוּל אֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם (al-gevul erets Edom) = "upon the border of the land of Edom" — Aaron dies at the boundary of the nation that refused Israel passage, within sight of the land of his brother-nation. The geographical detail heightens the narrative poignancy. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων ἐν Ωρ τῷ ὄρει ἐπὶ τῶν ὁρίων γῆς Εδωμ λέγων ("and [the] Lord said to Moses and Aaron at Hor the mountain upon the borders of the land of Edom, saying:").

**24** "Shall-be-gathered [ye'asef] Aaron [Aharon] unto [el] his-people [ammav], for [ki] not [lo] he-shall-come [yavo] unto [el] the-land [ha-arets] which [asher] I-have-given [natatti] to-the-Sons of [li-venei] Israel [Yisra'el], because [al] that [asher] you-rebelled [meritem] [et] my-mouth [pi] at-the-waters of [le-mei] Meribah [Merivah].

> **Key terms:** יֵאָסֵף אַהֲרֹן אֶל־עַמָּיו (ye'asef Aharon el-ammav) = "Aaron shall be gathered to his people" — the euphemism for death used of the patriarchs (Gen 25:8, 17; 35:29; 49:29, 33). It implies reunion with ancestors in Sheol. עַל אֲשֶׁר מְרִיתֶם אֶת־פִּי (al asher meritem et-pi) = "because you rebelled [against] my mouth" — the hiphil of מרה (marah, "to rebel/be bitter"), the same root as the name Meribah. Moses and Aaron "rebelled against the mouth/command" of YHWH — a severe charge. The plural מְרִיתֶם (meritem, "you [pl.] rebelled") indicts both leaders. **LXX:** προστεθήτω Ααρων πρὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ, ὅτι οὐ μὴ εἰσέλθητε εἰς τὴν γῆν ἣν δέδωκα τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ, διότι παρωξύνατέ με ἐπὶ τοῦ ὕδατος τῆς λοιδορίας ("let Aaron be added to his people, for you shall not enter the land which I have given to the sons of Israel, because you provoked me at the water of reviling").

**25** Take [qakh] [et] Aaron [Aharon] and-[et] Eleazar [El'azar] his-son [beno], and-bring-them-up [ve-ha'al] [et]-them [otam] Mount [Hor] Hor [ha-Hor].

> **Key terms:** וְהַעַל אֹתָם הֹר הָהָר (ve-ha'al otam Hor ha-Har) = "and bring them up Mount Hor" — the hiphil imperative of עלה (alah, "to go up"). Moses must lead father and son up the mountain for the transfer of the priesthood — an ascent toward death for Aaron and toward office for Eleazar. **LXX:** λαβὲ τὸν Ααρων καὶ Ελεαζαρ τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ καὶ ἀναβίβασον αὐτοὺς εἰς Ωρ τὸ ὄρος ("take Aaron and Eleazar his son and bring them up to Hor the mountain").

**26** And-strip [ve-hafsheet] [et] Aaron [Aharon] [et] his-garments [begadav], and-clothe [ve-hilbashta] [et]-them [otam] [et] Eleazar [El'azar] his-son [beno]; and-Aaron [ve-Aharon] shall-be-gathered [ye'asef] and-shall-die [u-met] there [sham]."

> **Key terms:** וְהַפְשֵׁט אֶת־אַהֲרֹן אֶת־בְּגָדָיו (ve-hafsheet et-Aharon et-begadav) = "and strip Aaron [of] his garments" — the hiphil imperative of פשׁט (pashat, "to strip off"). The priestly vestments — symbol of office, identity, and divine appointment — are to be physically transferred. וְהִלְבַּשְׁתָּ אֹתָם אֶת־אֶלְעָזָר (ve-hilbashta otam et-El'azar) = "and you shall clothe them [upon] Eleazar" — the same garments are placed on the son. This is the only recorded investiture of a high priest after the original consecration of Aaron (Exod 28–29; Lev 8). The transfer is immediate: the priesthood passes without interruption. וְאַהֲרֹן יֵאָסֵף וּמֵת שָׁם (ve-Aharon ye'asef u-met sham) = "and Aaron shall be gathered and shall die there" — the death is foretold with finality. **LXX:** καὶ ἔκδυσον Ααρων τὴν στολὴν αὐτοῦ καὶ ἔνδυσον Ελεαζαρ τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ· καὶ Ααρων προστεθεὶς ἀποθανέτω ἐκεῖ ("and strip Aaron of his robe and clothe Eleazar his son; and let Aaron, being gathered, die there").

**27** And-did [va-ya'as] Moses [Mosheh] as-which [ka'asher] commanded [tsivvah] YHWH; and-they-went-up [va-ya'alu] unto [el] Mount [Hor] Hor [ha-Hor] before-the-eyes of [le-einei] all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּעַשׂ מֹשֶׁה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה (va-ya'as Mosheh ka'asher tsivvah YHWH) = "and Moses did as YHWH commanded" — the obedience formula, contrasting implicitly with the disobedience at Meribah (v. 11). לְעֵינֵי כָּל־הָעֵדָה (le-einei kol-ha-edah) = "before the eyes of all the congregation" — the ascent is public. The entire community witnesses the three figures climbing the mountain, knowing that only two will return. **LXX:** καὶ ἐποίησεν Μωυσῆς καθὰ συνέταξεν κύριος, καὶ ἀνεβίβασεν αὐτοὺς εἰς Ωρ τὸ ὄρος ἔναντι πάσης τῆς συναγωγῆς ("and Moses did as [the] Lord commanded, and he brought them up to Hor the mountain before all the congregation").

**28** And-stripped [va-yafsheet] Moses [Mosheh] [et] Aaron [Aharon] [et] his-garments [begadav], and-clothed [va-yalbesh] [et]-them [otam] [et] Eleazar [El'azar] his-son [beno]; and-died [va-yamot] Aaron [Aharon] there [sham] on-the-top of [be-rosh] the-mountain [ha-har]; and-came-down [va-yered] Moses [Mosheh] and-Eleazar [ve-El'azar] from [min] the-mountain [ha-har].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּפְשֵׁט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־אַהֲרֹן (va-yafsheet Mosheh et-Aharon) = "and Moses stripped Aaron" — Moses performs the act with his own hands, undressing his brother for death. The scene is extraordinarily intimate: the older brother disrobes the younger, re-clothes his nephew, and witnesses the death. בְּרֹאשׁ הָהָר (be-rosh ha-har) = "on the top of the mountain" — Aaron dies at the summit, in the liminal space between earth and heaven. The detail parallels Moses' own eventual death on Mount Nebo (Deut 34:1–5). וַיֵּרֶד מֹשֶׁה וְאֶלְעָזָר מִן־הָהָר (va-yered Mosheh ve-El'azar min-ha-har) = "and Moses and Eleazar came down from the mountain" — two ascended as priests; one descends as priest, one as prophet. Aaron remains on the summit. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξέδυσεν Μωυσῆς τὸν Ααρων τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐνέδυσεν αὐτὰ Ελεαζαρ τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ· καὶ ἀπέθανεν Ααρων ἐπὶ τῆς κορυφῆς τοῦ ὄρους· καὶ κατέβη Μωυσῆς καὶ Ελεαζαρ ἐκ τοῦ ὄρους ("and Moses stripped Aaron of his garments and clothed them [upon] Eleazar his son; and Aaron died on the top of the mountain; and Moses and Eleazar came down from the mountain").

**29** And-saw [va-yir'u] all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah] that [ki] had-expired [gava] Aaron [Aharon]; and-they-wept [va-yivku] [et] Aaron [Aharon] thirty [sheloshim] day[s] [yom], all [kol] the-house of [beit] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** כִּי גָוַע אַהֲרֹן (ki gava Aharon) = "that Aaron had expired" — the verb גָּוַע (gava, "to expire/breathe one's last") is the same word the people used in their complaint in v. 3 (גָּוַעְנוּ, gava'nu, "we had expired"). The narrative creates a grim echo: the people wished they had expired; now Aaron has. וַיִּבְכּוּ אֶת־אַהֲרֹן שְׁלֹשִׁים יוֹם (va-yivku et-Aharon sheloshim yom) = "and they wept for Aaron thirty days" — a full month of mourning, the same duration later observed for Moses (Deut 34:8). This stands in sharp contrast to the absence of any mourning notation for Miriam (v. 1). כָּל־בֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל (kol-beit Yisra'el) = "all the house of Israel" — the entire nation mourns. Aaron, the man of peace (Avot 1:12: "loving peace and pursuing peace, loving the creatures and drawing them near to Torah"), is mourned universally. **LXX:** καὶ εἶδεν πᾶσα ἡ συναγωγὴ ὅτι ἀπελύθη Ααρων, καὶ ἔκλαυσαν τὸν Ααρων τριάκοντα ἡμέρας πᾶς οἶκος Ισραηλ ("and all the congregation saw that Aaron had departed, and they wept for Aaron thirty days, all the house of Israel").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 172–173

1. **Parashah division:** A closed section marker (ס) precedes 20:1 in the Leningrad Codex, connecting this chapter to the preceding red heifer legislation (ch. 19) within the same parashah חֻקַּת (Khuqqat, "statute of," Num 19:1–22:1). An open section marker (פ) appears before v. 14, marking the Edom embassy as a distinct literary unit within the parashah.

2. **The laconic report of Miriam's death (v. 1):** The Leningrad Codex preserves the terse two-clause report — וַתָּמָת שָׁם מִרְיָם וַתִּקָּבֵר שָׁם ("and Miriam died there and she was buried there") — without elaboration. No age is given (contrast Aaron's age in Num 33:39, one hundred twenty-three years), no mourning period is specified, and no eulogistic formula appears. The contrast with Aaron's thirty-day mourning (v. 29) is stark and has generated extensive rabbinic and modern commentary.

3. **The singular יָדוֹ (yado, "his hand") in v. 11:** The Leningrad Codex clearly reads the singular יָדוֹ (yado), not the dual/plural יָדָיו (yadav, "his hands"). The Samaritan Pentateuch also reads the singular. Some medieval manuscripts and the LXX (τὴν χεῖρα αὐτοῦ, "his hand") confirm the singular reading. The singular may indicate one decisive act of striking, though the verse specifies פַּעֲמָיִם (pa'amayim, "twice").

4. **הַמֹּרִים (ha-morim, "the rebels") in v. 10:** The Leningrad vocalization with holem on the mem confirms the reading as the hiphil participle of מרה (marah, "to rebel"), not מורים from ירה (yarah, "to teach"). The rabbis (Midrash Bemidbar Rabbah 19:9) explored both possibilities, but the Masoretic vocalization settles the reading as "rebels."

5. **The divine speech formula in v. 23:** YHWH speaks to both Moses and Aaron jointly (אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל־אַהֲרֹן), yet the content concerns Aaron's death and Eleazar's succession. Aaron hears the pronouncement of his own death — a detail of remarkable narrative weight.

6. **הֹר הָהָר (Hor ha-Har, vv. 22, 23, 25, 27):** The toponym appears four times in this chapter, always with the definite article on הָהָר. The Leningrad Codex consistently vocalizes הֹר with holem (not holam male), indicating a proper noun distinct from the common noun הַר (har, "mountain"). The name may derive from a non-Semitic substrate language or may be an archaic Hebrew formation meaning "the mountain-mountain," i.e., "the mountain par excellence."

### LXX Divergences (Codex Vaticanus)

7. **ἀπελύθη for גָּוַע (v. 29):** Where the MT uses גָּוַע (gava, "expired/breathed his last"), the Vaticanus LXX renders ἀπελύθη (apelythe, "was released/departed"), a gentler, more dignified expression. The Greek avoids the starkness of the Hebrew death-language, perhaps reflecting Hellenistic sensibilities about describing a priestly leader's death.

8. **ἐπὶ τῆς κορυφῆς τοῦ ὄρους for בְּרֹאשׁ הָהָר (v. 28):** The LXX renders בְּרֹאשׁ (be-rosh, "on the head/top of") with κορυφή (koryphe, "peak/summit"), a precise and literary Greek equivalent that captures the mountaintop setting.

9. **λοιδορία for מְרִיבָה (v. 24):** The Vaticanus renders מְרִיבָה (Merivah, "contention") as λοιδορία (loidoria, "reviling/abuse"), interpreting the place-name's meaning rather than transliterating it. This is consistent with the LXX's tendency to translate Hebrew toponyms that carry theological weight, while in v. 13 it uses ἀντιλογία (antilogia, "contradiction/dispute").

10. **The LXX addition in v. 19:** The Vaticanus text of v. 19 renders "by the highway" (בַּמְּסִלָּה, ba-mesillah) as παρὰ τὸ ὄρος (para to oros, "by the mountain"), a geographical rather than infrastructural reference. This may reflect a different Vorlage or an interpretive adaptation to the mountainous terrain of the region.

### Theological and Structural Observations

11. **The death-frame structure:** The chapter opens with Miriam's death (v. 1) and closes with Aaron's death (vv. 28–29), creating an inclusio of mortality that frames the central crisis. The three siblings of the exodus — Miriam, Aaron, Moses — are all under the shadow of death in this chapter. Miriam dies, Aaron dies, and Moses receives the sentence that he too will not enter the land (v. 12). The old order is passing; the new generation (represented by Eleazar) is ascending.

12. **Water as connective motif:** Water binds the chapter's disparate episodes: Miriam dies and the water ceases (v. 1–2); the people demand water (vv. 3–5); water comes from the rock (v. 11); the place is named for the water-strife (v. 13); Israel promises not to drink Edom's water (vv. 17, 19). The מֵי מְרִיבָה (mei Merivah, "waters of contention") becomes the name of the place and the cause of the leaders' exclusion from the land.

13. **The nature of Moses' sin at Meribah:** The text offers multiple possible explanations for Moses' transgression: (a) he struck the rock instead of speaking to it (v. 11 vs. v. 8); (b) he struck it twice, suggesting doubt (v. 11); (c) he used the first-person plural "shall we bring out" (v. 10), arrogating the miracle to himself and Aaron; (d) he called the people "rebels" in anger (v. 10); (e) the overarching charge is failure of faith — לֹא־הֶאֱמַנְתֶּם בִּי (lo-he'emantem bi, "you did not believe in me") — and failure to sanctify YHWH (v. 12). The multiplicity of interpretations (Rashi, Rambam, Ramban, and others each offer distinct readings) reflects the deliberate opacity of the text.

14. **The garment transfer (vv. 26, 28):** The stripping and re-clothing constitutes the only high-priestly succession narrated in the Torah. The act is performed by Moses — the prophet invests the priest — establishing the principle that prophetic authority mediates priestly appointment. The garments (בְּגָדִים, begadim) are the same sacred vestments described in Exodus 28–29 and Leviticus 8: the ephod, the breastpiece, the robe, the turban with its golden plate. Their transfer from father to son, performed on a mountaintop beside a dying man, is among the most solemn scenes in the Torah.

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folio 172r–173r, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pages 127–128. All transliterations follow the EMET standard. YHWH is transliterated, not translated. Conjunctions are preserved. Numbers rendered in English words.*
